figures.caption: Table 6: Hop-wise downstream QA performance with two first-stage retrievers. We report EM / F1 (%) for each hop category and the macro-average across available hops.

Figure 1: Summary of our main empirical findings. Methods involving SetCE or ParaSet are our set-level retrieval methods. (a) Set-level methods improve QA performance in several settings, and combining ParaSet+SetCE with document-level CE yields the strongest results. (b) Macro-average QA EM using Qwen3 as the first-stage retriever, decomposed by overlap with CE5. Compared with CE10, (ParaSet + SetCE)+CE5 recovers more CE5-missed examples and achieves higher overall EM, indicating complementary evidence recovery. See § 4.2 and § 4.3.

Table 3: Downstream QA EM (%) with CE augmentation across answer-generation LLMs. For each model M , the CE-augmented variant M+CE5 combines the method’s top-ranked set with CE5 passages. For CE, augmentation corresponds to CE10, i.e., expanding the returned passages from top-5 to 10.
Table 13: Comparison between multi-step (agentic) and single-step retrieval settings on the MuSiQue dataset. We report QA EM for each hop category and the macro average.

Figure 5: Retrieval F5 versus latency across HotpotQA, 2WikiMultihopQA, and MuSiQue using Contriever as the first-stage retriever. Each curve varies the number of first-stage candidate passages with K ∈ {5, 10, . . . , 50}, and each point corresponds to one value of K, left-to-right order. The x-axis reports per-query latency on a logarithmic scale; for all methods except Bi, we exclude the first-stage bi-encoder retrieval time.
Table 12: Standalone QA performance of ParaSet. We report EM / F1 (%) grouped by hop count, along with macro-average performance.
Table 7: Inference latency in seconds per query for different retrieval and reranking pipelines. K denotes the number of first-stage candidate passages.
Table 9: Comparison with an LLM-based set retrieval method. We report downstream QA EM / F1 (%).
